1: The daughters of Zelophehad appear at a critical moment: the census of ch26 has just assigned land by family heads with male names, and no provision exists for a man who dies with daughters but no sons. The five daughters — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah — are named as individuals, unusually specific in a text that often refers to women collectively or anonymously. Their full genealogy (Zelophehad → Hepher → Gilead → Machir → Manasseh) establishes their legitimate lineage within a major tribe, giving their claim maximum legal standing.
2: They stand before (wayyaʿămōḏnâ lip̄nê) Moses, Eleazar, the leaders, and the whole congregation — the full public judicial assembly at the sanctuary entrance. Ancient legal proceedings required public witnesses; presenting the claim before the entire assembled community transforms a family dispute into constitutional law. The location — the tent of meeting's entrance — places the claim in the divine court, not merely before human judges.
3: The daughters establish two facts: (1) their father died from his own sin, not from Korah's rebellion (bəʿăḏat Qōraḥ lōʾ-hāyâ); and (2) he left no sons. The first point matters legally: Korah's rebels and their households were destroyed (yibbālēʿ, 16:33), which may have forfeited their inheritance rights entirely. Zelophehad's ordinary death in the wilderness keeps his lineage's land claim intact; only the succession mechanism is missing.
4: The daughters' argument: lāmmâ yiggāraʿ šem-ʾāḇînû mittôk mišpaḥtô — why should our father's name be diminished from his clan? The inheritance of land is the perpetuation of family identity; land without an heir means the family name is erased from tribal memory. The appeal is not purely economic but genealogical-covenantal: every family was to have its place in the tribal inheritance of the promised land, and that place should not disappear due to an accident of birth.
5: wayyaqrēḇ Moshe ʾet-mišpāṭān lip̄nê YHWH — Moses brought their case before YHWH. The verb qāraḇ (draw near) is used for priestly approach; Moses here acts as the supreme legal mediator. He does not decide the case himself but refers it to divine judgment. This referral pattern — used also in the Sabbath-breaker case (Num 15:34) and the Passover impurity case (Num 9:8) — shows the development of Mosaic case law as responsive divine revelation rather than systematic pre-established code.
6: YHWH's direct response through Moses establishes the divine validation of the daughters' claim. The legislative response is in two parts: an immediate ruling for this specific case (v7) and a general inheritance law for future cases (vv8-11). The specific case generates the general principle — characteristic of the casuistic (case-law) legislative style of P material throughout Numbers.
7: kēn ḇənôt Ṣəlop̄ḥāḏ dōḇərôt — the daughters of Zelophehad speak rightly. The divine affirmation is unequivocal. YHWH does not merely permit their request; he declares them to be right. The inheritance shall pass to them, and their father's possession shall transfer through them. The ruling is immediately implemented: give them a hereditary possession among their father's brothers.
8: The general rule: if a man dies without a son, his inheritance passes to his daughter. The rule creates a hierarchy: son → daughter → brothers → father's brothers → nearest clan relative. This hierarchy expands the inheritance options well beyond the default male-primogeniture norm, while maintaining patrilineal structure. ANE parallel: Code of Hammurabi §§171-174 allowed daughters to inherit as malkatum in certain circumstances; Middle Assyrian Laws §§29-33 addressed daughter-inheritance from childless fathers. Israel's ruling goes further in explicitly validating daughters' claims as equal to sons' in the absence of male heirs.
9: If no daughter exists, inheritance passes to the brothers. The sequence ensures that land remains within the closest possible kinship circle before passing to more distant relatives — land was not simply property but the material form of covenant inheritance, the family's portion in the promised land. The loss of land to unrelated parties would represent a covenant failure.
10: If no brothers, to the father's brothers (uncles). The kinship succession model mirrors the gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer) pattern of Leviticus 25 and Ruth, where the closest available male relative had both the right and obligation to redeem family property. The inheritance succession law and the redemption institution are complementary structures for keeping land within its covenant-assigned family lines.
11: If no father's brothers, to the nearest relative in the family. The phrase lišʾērô haqqārōḇ ʾēlāyw — to the one nearest to him from his family — allows for the extension of inheritance to any identifiable family connection rather than letting the land revert to tribal common land. This is declared a ḥuqqat mišpāṭ — a statute of judgment — permanent for all Israel. The case of Zelophehad's daughters thus became constitutional inheritance law, cited later in Josh 17:3-6 when they claimed their allotment.
12: YHWH commands Moses to ascend the Abarim mountains — the range culminating in Nebo/Pisgah, from whose height Canaan is visible. The viewing of the land before death is structured as a gift within the judgment: Moses cannot enter, but he may see. The land remains as real and promised as ever; Moses's personal exclusion does not diminish the promise's certainty for the generation that will cross.
13: Moses will be gathered to his people (wəneʾesap̄tā ʾel-ʿammekā) as Aaron was gathered to his (20:24-29). The parallel construction frames Moses's death as a completion parallel to Aaron's — the two founding leaders of the exodus generation will die in the same wilderness, by the same divine appointment, before the same land they served toward. The phrase marks death as a covenant homecoming rather than mere biological ending.
14: The reason for Moses's exclusion is stated again: rebellion against YHWH's command at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh. The specific charge — mərîtem ʾet-pî — you rebelled against my mouth — uses the word for speaking against divine command. The phrase ləhaqdîšênî — to sanctify/display my holiness — restates Num 20:12's verdict: Moses's failure was not merely disobedience but a failure to communicate YHWH's character accurately before the congregation.
15: Moses's response to his own death sentence is to pray for a successor — not to appeal the verdict, not to grieve, but to intercede for the community he has led. This self-forgetting intercession after personal judgment defines Moses's character: the man who cannot enter the land still cares more about who will lead the people into it than about his own fate.
16: yip̄qōḏ YHWH ʾĕlōhê hārûḥōt ləkol-bāśār ʾîš ʿal-hāʿēḏâ — may YHWH, the God of the spirits of all flesh, appoint a man over the congregation. The address of God as ʾĕlōhê hārûḥōt ləkol-bāśār — the God who breathed life into all flesh — invokes the creator's authority over life and its Spirit, grounding Moses's request in the deepest possible theological claim: the one who gives the spirit of life to all humans can choose who carries his spirit for leading.
17: The qualifications for the successor: ʾăšer-yēṣēʾ lip̄nêhem wǎʾăšer yāḇōʾ lip̄nêhem — one who will go out before them and come in before them. The military leadership idiom (yāṣāʾ... bāʾ before the people) describes a commander who personally leads in battle rather than directing from the rear. The pastoral dimension follows: wƏʾăšer yôṣîʾēm wǎʾăšer yəḇîʾēm — who will lead them out and bring them in. Moses's concern is not succession of position but succession of shepherding character. The phrase ʾăšer lōʾ-tihyeh ʿăḏat YHWH kaṣṣōʾn ʾăšer ʾên-lāhem rōʿeh — so that YHWH's congregation will not be like sheep without a shepherd — became the canonical shepherd-without-leader image used by Ezekiel (34:5), Zechariah (10:2), and Jesus (Matt 9:36; Mark 6:34).
18: YHWH designates Joshua son of Nun — previously Moses's attendant (Exod 24:13; 33:11; Num 11:28) and one of the two faithful spies (Num 14:6-9,30,38) — as the successor. The qualification: ʾîš ʾăšer-rûaḥ bô — a man in whom the Spirit resides. This Sprit-qualification echoes the description of the 70 elders (Num 11:25-29) and anticipates the prophetic-Spirit theme in Joshua and the Judges. The laying on of hands (səmiḵâ) is the transfer gesture; in rabbinic Judaism this became the standard ordination ceremony (Sanhedrin 13b).
19: The commissioning must happen before Eleazar the priest and the whole congregation — public, witnessed, communal. The transparency of the succession prevents later disputes: everyone who will follow Joshua has witnessed his divine appointment. This public commissioning pattern becomes the template for subsequent leadership transitions: Moses commissions Joshua publicly (Deut 31:7-8,23), Samuel anoints David publicly (1 Sam 16:13), and the apostolic laying-on-of-hands in Acts follows the same communal principle.
20: wənātattā mêhôḏəkā ʿālāyw — transfer some of your authority/majesty to him. The word הוֹד (hôḏ) — splendor, majesty, authority — describes the divine radiance that characterizes legitimate authority. Moses does not transfer all his authority (v21: Joshua will consult the Urim, while Moses spoke face to face) but enough for the congregation to obey. The partial transfer creates a hierarchy: Joshua is legitimate successor but not Moses's functional equal.
21: Joshua's consultative mode: he shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who will inquire for him by the אוּרִים (ûrîm) — the oracular objects kept by the high priest (Exod 28:30; Lev 8:8). The Urim and Thummim provided binary yes/no decisions in judicial and military matters; the high priest mediated the divine answer. Moses had direct divine access (YHWH spoke with him mouth-to-mouth, Num 12:8); Joshua operates through priestly mediation. This structural distinction marks the post-Mosaic period as one of more mediated revelation — a pattern that will characterize the entire Judges-Kings-Prophets era.
22: Moses executes the commissioning with full compliance: takes Joshua, presents him before Eleazar and the congregation. The compliance formula (kaʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe) marks this as another act of covenantal obedience. In the act of commissioning his own successor, Moses models the humility of covenant leadership: the leader serves the community's future, not his own position's perpetuation.
23: The laying on of hands and commissioning complete the succession ceremony. Numbers ends in ch36; this appointment in ch27 functions as the theological turning point: from here, the narrative moves toward Moses's death and Joshua's entry. The ch27 Zelophehad ruling and Joshua commissioning are paired as law and leadership — the two things Israel needs most as it enters the land: just property law and capable, spirit-filled leadership.
